preamble: The palatalized voiced velar plosive /ɡʲ/, often arising from the palatalization of a plain velar /ɡ/ before front vowels or the palatal approximant /j/, can undergo a series of phonetic changes leading to a voiced alveolar affricate /dz/. This transformation typically involves a combination of fronting, where the articulation moves from the velar to the alveolar region, and affrication, where the plosive release develops into a fricative component.
phoneticEffects: Fronting (velar to alveolar), Affrication (plosive to affricate)

examples.note: Palatalized g (ǵ) shifted to z, which was pronounced with or without affrication, including [dz], especially from Latin clusters like -RǴ-, -LǴ-, and -NǴ-.
As a reflex of the second regressive velar palatalization in Common Slavic, where palatalized g' evolved to the affricate dz.
Velar stop /ɡ/ affricates to [d͡z] in the vicinity of high front vowels, implying an intermediate palatalized stage.
The plosive [g] developed into dz after n (i.e., -ng-) and in the geminate -gg-, and initially before front vowels.

sources: Malkiel, Y. (1975). Directions for Historical Linguistics: A Symposium.
Britannica. Slavic languages - Indo-European, Dialects, Grammar.
Bremmer Jr., R. H. (2009). An Introduction to Old Frisian. John Benjamins Publishing Company.
